original_abstract: The long-recognized potential for the cultural and creative industries (CCIs) to drive national growth has transitioned from the UK’s landmark 1998 mapping report to contemporary "sector deals" integrated into overarching industrial strategies. This chapter investigates how these dominant CCI policy narratives manifest within local governance by examining two city-regional pilot programs for Local Industrial Strategies (LIS) in the West Midlands and the North West of England. Utilizing discourse analysis, the study scrutinizes the social practices and rhetorical flourishes that define "the local," uncovering the complex boundary work—the conceptual distinctions used by policy actors to categorize space and agency—inherent in the planning process. The findings highlight a shift toward contractualism between central and local authorities. Following Paasi’s geographic frameworks, the chapter identifies specific discursive interactions that delineate the borders of these city-regions, ultimately demonstrating the significant power of "place" to exert agency even within centrally mandated policy instruments designed for sub-national economic development.
humanized_abstract: Since the influential mapping report of 1998, it has been commonly acknowledged that cultural and creative industries (CCIs) present considerable opportunities for other countries to achieve national development. This agenda-holding role has travelled from the Dust & Glue 1998 mapping report through to the present iteration of sector deals as part of an industrial strategy. In this chapter, we delve further into how local governance plays out the prevailing policy agenda on CCIs in the context of two of the six city-region pilot sites for the development of Local Industrial Strategies (LIS) in the West Midlands and the North West of England. Employing a discourse analytical approach, we reveal the ways of doing and words and phrases that constitute “the local” in the context of boundary work and hence discover what is involved in the planning of city-regions under present policy conditions. We reveal a move from the developmental policies which had been associated with the concept of creative industries towards contractual relations between the centre and localities. Again using Paasi’s work on borders and boundary work, we describe the discourses used to draw boundary lines around the cities and regions considered relevant to policy-makers and conclude with a consideration of the powerful presence of place in policies and processes devised from the centre with the aim of bringing about change at the sub-national level.
